location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:166 (doResolveEscalation fallbackArgs, '--decision') and :247-250 (doRecordHumanGate args, '--evidence') / src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts:26-35 (validateFeatureSlug), :47-59 (requireString/requireNonEmptyString) — REQ-008, REQ-010, REQ-011
claim: The dispatcher passes human-authored free text (`decision` on the gatekeeper resolve-escalation fallback path, `evidence` on recordHumanGate) directly as a raw, un-encoded CLI argument VALUE to Orky's own gatekeeper CLI. Read C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 30-41): it scans the ENTIRE argv array for any token starting with `--`, not merely the token immediately following a recognized flag name, and if the token FOLLOWING a `--flag` also starts with `--`, it sets that flag's value to boolean `true` (does NOT consume it) and then re-scans the following token itself as an independent new flag. Because `decision`/`evidence` are always the LAST element of their respective argv arrays (`['resolve-escalation','--feature',featureDir,'--id',escalationId,'--decision',decision]` and `['record','--feature',featureDir,'--gate',gate,'--verdict',verdict,'--evidence',evidence]`), any value beginning with `--` is reinterpreted as a CLI flag instead of being carried through as the literal text. Neither `orky-action-validate.ts`'s `validateResolveEscalationRequest`/`validateRecordHumanGateRequest` nor the dispatcher itself reject or sanitize a `decision`/`evidence` string starting with `--`. I reproduced this adversarially by copying `parseArgs` verbatim from `C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js` and feeding it the exact argv the dispatcher constructs (command run in this session, not against any real project): (1) `evidence:'--force'` for `recordHumanGate` with `gate:'human-review', verdict:'pass'` parses to `{ ..., evidence: true, force: true }` — i.e. the human's `evidence` text is silently DROPPED (replaced by boolean `true`) and the literal `--force` flag is injected into gatekeeper's own `record(feature, gate, verdict, evidence, force)` call (`cli.js:91`: `a.force === true || a.force === 'true'` evaluates to `true`), even though `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`'s own code/tests (TEST-242) assert 'the invocation never includes --force' — that assertion is about the literal ARRAY the dispatcher constructs, not about what the CLI's parser ultimately derives from a value inside that array, so it does not catch this. (2) `decision:'--anything is fine'` for the `resolveEscalation` gatekeeper fallback parses to `{ ..., decision: true, 'anything is fine': true }` — `gatekeeper.js:627` then executes `esc.decision = decision || ''` with `decision === true`, so `state.json`'s recorded `escalations[].decision` field is silently corrupted to the boolean `true` instead of the human's actual decision text. Impact: (a) DATA INTEGRITY — the human decision/evidence text this feature exists to durably and accurately record (the enterprise-arch concern, REQ-013's whole rationale) is silently lost/replaced for any input starting with `--`, which is not a contrived edge case (e.g. a reviewer typing '--- see PR #42 ---' or literally describing a CLI flag in their reasoning); (b) DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH DEGRADATION for REQ-008 — the spec explicitly requires the gate restriction to be enforced 'independent of (and in addition to) gatekeeper.js's own enforcement' specifically as defense-in-depth, and requires the invocation to 'NEVER pass --force'; this finding shows attacker/user-controlled `evidence` text CAN cause `--force` to reach `gatekeeper record` regardless of the dispatcher's own hard-coded args array. It is currently INERT only because `gate` is independently pre-validated to `{brainstorm,human-review}` before the CLI ever runs (so `record()`'s `!HUMAN_GATES.has(gate) && !force` bypass check is never reached in the false/true branch that matters) — i.e. safety today rests on ONE remaining correct layer, not the two independent layers REQ-008 says it wants; a future regression in the gate pre-check (the exact class of bug reviews exist to catch) would make this a live human-gate-restriction bypass, not merely a data-corruption bug. `feature`/`escalationId` are also passed as raw argv values (`--feature <slug>` in the feedback emit path, `--id <escalationId>` in the gatekeeper fallback) and are similarly reinterpretable if they start with `--`, though lower-likelihood in practice (`feature` must already resolve to an EXISTING `.orky/features/<slug>` directory before any CLI runs, and `--payload`'s JSON content was verified, by the same repro, to survive intact even when `feature` is corrupted — so this narrower sub-case mainly causes wrong feature-attribution in the emitted feedback event, not further injection). `title`/`detail`/`phase` (submitWork) are unaffected because they are always carried inside the JSON-encoded `--payload` argument, never as raw argv.
fix: Before constructing any argv array passed to `runOrkyCli`, reject (return `errorKind:'invalid-args'`, CONV-001-actionable) any free-text field that will be placed as a RAW (non-JSON-encoded) CLI argument value if it starts with `--`: specifically `decision` and `escalationId` in `validateResolveEscalationRequest`, `evidence` in `validateRecordHumanGateRequest`, and `feature`'s slug in `validateFeatureSlug` (all four flow into a raw argv position in `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`). A minimal, precise guard is `if (value.startsWith('--')) return { ok:false, error: '<field> must not start with "--" (would be misread as a CLI flag)' }` in each of those validators, or centralize it as a shared `rejectFlagLike(field, value)` helper reused by all of them. Add a regression test asserting `evidence:'--force'` and `decision:'--anything'` (and a `feature`/`escalationId` value starting with `--`) are rejected with `invalid-args` before any CLI is spawned. Longer-term, note in `docs/features/orky-action-dispatch.md` that any FUTURE raw (non-JSON) argv field added to this dispatcher must go through the same guard, since `execFile`'s argument-array safety (REQ-010) prevents SHELL injection but does not prevent the downstream CLI's own naive flag-scanning `parseArgs` from reinterpreting a value that looks like a flag — a distinct vulnerability class (CWE-88, argument injection) that the array-based invocation alone does not close.
resolution: Verified fixed. src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts now has a shared `rejectFlagLike(field, value)` helper (`if (value.startsWith('--')) return {ok:false, error:"<field> must not start with '--' (reserved for CLI flags)"}`) wired into: `validateFeatureSlug` (covers `feature` for all four actions via `requireFeatureSlug`/the optional-feature branch in `validateSubmitWorkRequest`), `validateResolveEscalationRequest`'s `escalationId` and `decision` checks, and `validateRecordHumanGateRequest`'s optional `evidence` check. Traced every raw-argv call site in src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts that the original finding named: the resolveEscalation feedback-emit `--feature` (line 151), the gatekeeper-fallback `--id`/`--decision` (line 166), submitWork's optional emit `--feature` (line 202), and recordHumanGate's optional `--evidence` (line 249) -- all five now read from `v.req` (the validated struct), and every one of doResolveEscalation/doSubmitWork/doRecordHumanGate/doDriveStatus calls its validator FIRST and returns `invalid-args` immediately on `!v.ok`, so there is no code path where an unvalidated raw string reaches `runOrkyCli`. `gate`/`verdict` need no textual guard: `gate` is constrained to the fixed 2-element `HUMAN_GATES` set (`{brainstorm,human-review}`) by the dispatcher's own REQ-008 check before the args array is ever built, and `verdict` is enum-checked to exactly `'pass'|'fail'` in the validator -- neither can ever be attacker-influenced free text. `projectRoot` (used raw as `--app <projectRoot>`) isn't flag-guarded either, but is structurally safe: `checkRoot()` requires it to normalize-match an entry in `registry.roots()`, and every registered root passed through feature 0005's `validateRegistryRoot` -> `path.resolve()`, which always returns a filesystem-rooted absolute path (drive-letter or POSIX `/` prefix) and can therefore never start with `--`. Independently re-verified the exploit is closed by re-reading C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 30-41, unchanged) and C:/dev/Orky/plugin/feedback/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 26-37, same vulnerable shape) -- both scan for any whole argv token with `a.startsWith('--')`; `rejectFlagLike`'s `value.startsWith('--')` check exactly matches that recognition threshold (values containing but not starting with `--`, e.g. `'ok --force'`, are a single argv element under array-form `execFile` and are never re-split, so they were never actually reachable -- the guard is neither over- nor under-inclusive). Confirmed `evidence:'--force'` and `decision:'--anything is fine'` are now rejected with `invalid-args` by the validator before any CLI spawns, closing both the data-integrity loss and the REQ-008 defense-in-depth gap the finding described. Ran the frozen regression suite (TEST-283..290, plus the full validator/dispatcher/cli-locate suites, 87 tests) -- all pass, including TEST-290's negative/scope assertion that submitWork's `title`/`detail`/`phase` MAY start with `--` and are still accepted; confirmed this is CORRECT, not just passing, because those three fields travel only inside the `JSON.stringify(...)`-encoded `--payload` argument (which itself always starts with `{`, never `--`), never as their own raw argv element, so guarding them would be over-broad. No gaps found.

claim: The exact same 3-line helper `function isPlainObject(v: unknown): v is Record<string, unknown> { return typeof v === 'object' && v !== null && !Array.isArray(v) }` is independently defined THREE times across this feature's own files: once in the pure `src/shared/orky-action-result.ts` (guards the parsed CLI stdout JSON), once in the pure `src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts` (guards the raw request object), and once again in the main-process `src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts` (guards the raw request object inside `extractString`/`summarizeArgs` for the audit log). A repo-wide grep confirms no pre-existing shared `isPlainObject` utility exists elsewhere that this feature could have reused instead — all three are net-new to feature 0007. Two of the three copies (`orky-action-result.ts` and `orky-action-validate.ts`) are both already `src/shared/` pure modules, so the duplication isn't even justified by a main/renderer or pure/impure boundary.
fix: Extract the helper once — e.g. a tiny new `src/shared/is-plain-object.ts` (or have `orky-action-dispatcher.ts` and `orky-action-result.ts` both import it from `orky-action-validate.ts`, which is already a dependency of the dispatcher) — and delete the other two copies. Purely mechanical, no behavior change; add nothing to the public API surface.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-audit.ts:21 (action: string) and :28 (errorKind?: string) in OrkyActionAuditRecord; src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:333 (private recordAudit(action: string, ...)); compare src/shared/orky-action-result.ts:16 (export type DispatchAction = 'resolveEscalation'|'submitWork'|'recordHumanGate'|'driveStatus', already imported transitively) and src/shared/types.ts:403-413 (OrkyActionErrorKind union) and .orky/features/0007-orky-action-dispatch/03-plan.md TASK-005 ("errorKind?: OrkyActionErrorKind")
claim: `OrkyActionAuditRecord.action` and `OrkyActionAuditRecord.errorKind` are both typed as bare `string` even though a narrower literal-union type already exists for each concept and is used everywhere else in the feature: `DispatchAction` (the exact 4 action names, defined in `orky-action-result.ts` and used to type-check every `mapCliRunToResult(action, ...)` call site) and `OrkyActionErrorKind` (the 10-member discriminator used throughout `OrkyActionResult.errorKind`). `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`'s own `recordAudit(action: string, ...)` parameter is called with only the 4 literal action names today, and `record.errorKind = result.errorKind` assigns a real `OrkyActionErrorKind` value in, but the field's declared type silently widens it to `string`. This is also a direct divergence from the plan: 03-plan.md TASK-005 pins the audit record shape as `errorKind?: OrkyActionErrorKind` verbatim, not `errorKind?: string`. Net effect: a typo'd action name or errorKind string passed to `recordAudit`/into the audit record in a future edit would compile silently instead of being caught, for a log whose entire purpose (REQ-013, enterprise-arch attributability) depends on exactly these fields being reliably one of the known values.
fix: Import `DispatchAction` from `@shared/orky-action-result` and use it for both `OrkyActionAuditRecord.action` and `recordAudit`'s `action` parameter; import `OrkyActionErrorKind` from `@shared/types` (already imported by `orky-action-dispatcher.ts` indirectly via `OrkyActionResult`) and use it for `OrkyActionAuditRecord.errorKind`, matching the plan's own TASK-005 type spec exactly.

claim: The literal object shape `{ ok: false, path: null, dispatched: false, errorKind: <X>, error: <Y> }` (the uniform pre-CLI dispatcher-level rejection per REQ-002/REQ-014) is written out by hand, byte-for-byte identical apart from the errorKind/error values, 17 separate times across the four `do*` action methods — once each for `invalid-args` (x4), `root-not-allowed` (x4), the `feature-not-found`-shaped `fdir` failure (x4), `orky-cli-not-found` (x4, plus a 5th inline variant at line 164 covered by FINDING-QUAL-005), and once for `gate-not-allowed` (x1, doRecordHumanGate only). The task brief for this review specifically asked whether the validate-then-checkRoot-then-resolveFeatureDir-then-locateCli sequence's repetition across the four actions is acceptable because each action's exact sequence legitimately differs (per REQ-006/007/008/009) — the SEQUENCING does legitimately differ (confirmed: different checks run in different orders/counts per action), but the REJECTION OBJECT SHAPE at each step does not differ at all, and is exactly the kind of boilerplate a shared helper collapses without hiding any of the real per-action logic. This is the single largest, most complex file in the feature (per the review brief's own framing) and is exactly where a future edit (e.g. adding a new field required on every dispatcher-level rejection, such as a request-id for tracing) has the most surface area to update inconsistently — 17 near-duplicate literal sites is a lot to keep in sync by hand.
fix: Add a small private helper, e.g. `private reject(errorKind: OrkyActionErrorKind, error: string): OrkyActionResult { return { ok: false, path: null, dispatched: false, errorKind, error } }`, and replace all 17 call sites (`return { ok: false, path: null, dispatched: false, errorKind: 'invalid-args', error: v.error }` becomes `return this.reject('invalid-args', v.error)`, etc.). This does not change any returned value and does not touch the FROZEN test suite's assertions (same resulting object shape).

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:155,159,164,170,172 (doResolveEscalation), :208,212,222 (doSubmitWork), :254,256 (doRecordHumanGate) — 10 `as OrkyActionResult` casts; contrast :279-282 (doDriveStatus's structurally identical return literals, which need NO cast)
claim: Every return statement inside the three mutating actions' `this.queue.run(featureDir, async () => {...})` callbacks is force-cast with `as OrkyActionResult` (10 occurrences). `doDriveStatus`, which returns structurally identical object literals (same `{ok, path, dispatched, errorKind?, error?, data?, exitCode?}` shape) directly from the method body rather than from inside a `queue.run(...)` callback, needs no such cast (lines 279-282) — because those literals sit in a position with contextual typing from the method's own `Promise<OrkyActionResult>` return-type annotation, so TypeScript checks each literal against the real interface (e.g. `path: 'gatekeeper'` is checked against `OrkyActionPath`). The `queue.run(async () => {...})` callbacks have no such contextual type (the callback is passed as a bare `() => Promise<T>` with `T` inferred structurally from the literals themselves, and `queue.run`'s generic `<T>` return type is never pinned to `OrkyActionResult` at the call site), so without the `as` cast a literal such as `path: 'feedback'` would infer as the widened `string` and fail to satisfy `OrkyActionPath` — the cast is compensating for a missing type annotation rather than expressing an intentional narrowing. Net effect: TypeScript is NOT actually verifying that these 10 object literals conform to `OrkyActionResult` at compile time (a typo'd property name or a value of the wrong type inside one of these literals would be silently accepted), for exactly the branches whose `ok`/`errorKind`/`path` shape correctness CONV-001 and REQ-002 depend on most.
fix: Give the `queue.run(...)` callbacks an explicit return-type annotation, e.g. `this.queue.run(featureDir, async (): Promise<OrkyActionResult> => { ... })`, which lets each return statement's object literal be checked contextually against the real interface exactly like `doDriveStatus`'s already are — then remove all 10 `as OrkyActionResult` casts. No behavior change; strictly restores compile-time verification of these result shapes.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:162-165 (doResolveEscalation's gatekeeper-fallback CLI-not-found branch) vs. every other orky-cli-not-found return site (:148, :193, :244, :274); tests/main/orky-action-dispatcher.test.ts:380-405 (TEST-252/253, REQ-012's only orky-cli-not-found coverage)
claim: 02-spec.md REQ-012 states the uniform shape for a CLI-not-found rejection as `{ ok:false, path:null, dispatched:false, errorKind:'orky-cli-not-found', error:... }` (path:null, literally), and every one of the four other orky-cli-not-found return sites in this file (lines 148, 193, 244, 274) matches that exactly. The fifth site — inside doResolveEscalation's queue.run callback, reached when the FIRST feedback emit succeeds with mode:'noop' (feedback disabled) and the SECOND lookup, for the gatekeeper CLI needed by the fallback, then fails to locate — instead returns `path: 'feedback'` (line 164), diverging from the spec's literal shape for every other occurrence of this errorKind in the same file. This specific branch (feedback located + emitted successfully, THEN gatekeeper unresolved) is not exercised by any test in the FROZEN suite: TEST-252 (REQ-012, line 381) only covers 'feedback CLI itself unresolved' (fails before queue.run ever starts, matching the locateOrkyCli mock returning null for feedback), and TEST-253 (line 394) only covers 'gatekeeper CLI unresolved' for recordHumanGate/driveStatus, neither of which exercises resolveEscalation's nested fallback-side gatekeeper lookup. Whether path:'feedback' here is an intentional richer signal (feedback DID run) or an oversight is not resolvable from the spec text alone, but the divergence is real, untested, and worth a deliberate decision rather than an accidental one.
fix: Either (a) change line 164 to `path: null` to match REQ-012's literal spec shape and every sibling orky-cli-not-found branch in the file, or (b) if `path: 'feedback'` is intentional (to signal that feedback WAS reached and used before the fallback lookup failed), add a one-line comment explaining the deliberate divergence from the other four sites. Either way, add a regression test exercising this exact branch (feedback locateOrkyCli succeeds + emits mode:'noop', gatekeeper locateOrkyCli returns null) so the chosen behavior is pinned rather than accidentally reachable-but-unverified.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:288-293 (checkRoot uses normalizeProjectRoot) vs :307-316 (resolveFeatureDir builds featureDir from the RAW projectRoot via join) and :150/:196/:246 (queue.run(featureDir) keys on that string); src/main/orky/orky-action-queue.ts:17-21 — REQ-015, REQ-004, CONV-010
claim: The per-featureDir serialization queue (REQ-015, the state.json lost-update guard) is keyed on the raw, un-normalized featureDir string join(projectRoot, '.orky', 'features', slug) built from the renderer-supplied projectRoot, while root MEMBERSHIP is checked case-insensitively via normalizeProjectRoot (checkRoot). REQ-004 and TEST-230 EXPLICITLY accept a projectRoot spelled with divergent case relative to the allowlisted entry. On win32 — the only supported platform — path.join normalizes slash style but PRESERVES case (verified: join('C:/dev/Proj',...) = 'C:\\dev\\Proj\\.orky\\features\\X' vs join('c:/dev/proj',...) = 'c:\\dev\\proj\\.orky\\features\\X' — distinct strings). So two concurrent mutating actions on the SAME physical feature submitted with case-divergent projectRoot spellings (both pass checkRoot) produce DIFFERENT OrkyActionQueue map keys and therefore do NOT serialize against each other. Both then invoke gatekeeper record / resolve-escalation, each doing a non-atomic read-modify-write of the SAME state.json (Windows' case-insensitive FS resolves both --feature paths to one file) → a lost update: the exact failure mode REQ-015 exists to prevent. This contradicts REQ-015's MUST ('mutating actions targeting the SAME resolved featureDir MUST be serialized' — the two ARE the same resolved featureDir, differing only by an accepted spelling) and violates CONV-010 (the dispatcher mixes a normalizeProjectRoot-folded key for membership with a source-dependent raw join() string for the queue/CLI key — the precise anti-pattern CONV-010 forbids). It is silent: no error is surfaced; a human's gate verdict or escalation decision is simply lost. TEST-264 pins serialization only for two calls that share the identical featureDir string, so the divergent-spelling hole is untested.
fix: Derive the queue key (and the featureDir handed to the CLI) from a normalized/resolved root rather than the raw renderer string — e.g. build featureDir on top of the canonical root checkRoot already resolves, or key OrkyActionQueue on normalizeProjectRoot(featureDir), reusing the SAME shared normalizer the dispatcher already imports for checkRoot (CONV-010). Add a regression test extending TEST-264: two concurrent recordHumanGate calls on the same feature with case-divergent projectRoot spellings MUST serialize (2nd CLI awaits the 1st) and both land in state.json with no lost write.
resolution: Verified fixed (ESC-004 approved fix). All three mutating queue.run(...) call sites in src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts now key the per-featureDir serialization queue by normalizeProjectRoot(...) -- the SAME case/slash-folding normalizer checkRoot() already uses, imported from ./validate-root: doResolveEscalation (line 150, this.queue.run(normalizeProjectRoot(featureDir), ...)), doSubmitWork (lines 195-196, queueKey = normalizeProjectRoot(featureDir ?? projectRoot) then this.queue.run(queueKey, ...), covering BOTH the feature-scoped and the no-feature project-level fallback key), and doRecordHumanGate (line 246, this.queue.run(normalizeProjectRoot(featureDir), ...)). driveStatus is read-only and correctly still bypasses the queue entirely. Confirmed the CLI argv VALUES are UNCHANGED -- no raw-argv position was touched: the emit '--app projectRoot'/'--feature feature' args (lines 151, 200-204), the gatekeeper-fallback 'resolve-escalation --feature featureDir' (line 166), and record's '--feature featureDir' (line 248) all still pass the original case-preserved projectRoot/featureDir, never the normalized form; ONLY the in-process Map key changed. Independently corroborated by the FROZEN assertions TEST-233/235/242 (which pin the exact CLI arg arrays to join(root,...) in the ORIGINAL mixed-case mkdtemp path) still passing on win32 -- had the fix accidentally normalized/lowercased the CLI-passed featureDir, those three would have gone RED. The OrkyActionQueue (orky-action-queue.ts) is keyed solely by the string the dispatcher hands it, so with all 3 sites normalized every same-physical-feature key now collapses. Read TEST-291/292/293/294 in full: each fix-target test builds a divergent spelling by upper-casing the root AND flipping back-slashes to forward-slashes (so it is BOTH case- and slash-divergent), fires two concurrent mutating calls -- one with the raw root, one with the divergent spelling -- on the SAME physical feature, and asserts the second CLI call does NOT start until the first is released (expect(order).toEqual(['first-start']) at the 30ms checkpoint, then the full serialized order after release). TEST-291 exercises doRecordHumanGate, TEST-292 doResolveEscalation's gatekeeper-fallback path (emit returns mode:'noop' to force the fallback), TEST-293 doSubmitWork's no-feature project-level fallback key, and TEST-294 is the same-spelling no-regression sanity mirroring TEST-264 -- one test per call site, so a partial fix (only 1-2 of the 3 sites normalized) would still leave at least one RED. Ran npx vitest run tests/main/orky-action-dispatcher.test.ts -> 46/46 green, and full npm test -> 140 files / 1037 tests green, no regression. Adversarially swept src/main/orky/ for the same bug class (a path-derived mutation key that could diverge by case/slash for the same physical path): the only other mutation-serialization structure is feature 0005's OrkyRegistry, which ALREADY routes every membership/mutation key through resolveCanonical/normalizeProjectRoot (its own FINDING-DA-001), with the statusByRoot key alignment explicitly documented; the remaining path/id-keyed maps (orky-tracker.ts and orky-stream-status.ts keyed by paneId; orky-root-engine.ts keyed by consumerId/orkyDir) are READ-ONLY watcher bookkeeping where a key divergence at worst duplicates a chokidar watcher, not a lost-update write race. No new instance of this bug class found; no new finding filed. REQ-015's MUST ('mutating actions targeting the SAME resolved featureDir MUST be serialized') is now genuinely satisfied for case/slash-divergent-but-physically-identical projectRoot spellings, closing the CONV-010 violation the finding described.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:158-159 (doResolveEscalation feedback branch), :211-212 (doSubmitWork feedback branch); src/shared/types.ts OrkyActionResult.dispatched; 02-spec.md REQ-002 / REQ-006 / REQ-007 / Open questions #2
claim: The feedback-path dispatched:true over-promises. REQ-002 defines dispatched:true as 'the human input landed durably (a live feedback emit, or a completed gatekeeper mutation)', treating the two as equivalent — but they are not equivalent in EFFECT. Verified against Orky source: feedback emit (C:/dev/Orky/plugin/feedback/cli.js:51-67 + feedback.js fileChannel/httpChannel:123-189) only writes the decision/work EVENT to the OUTBOX (file mode: <root>/.orky/feedback/outbox/; http mode: POSTs to the control-plane events endpoint). It does NOT resolve the escalation. Resolution happens later, asynchronously, in a SEPARATE running orchestrator's `feedback apply` tick, which reads the INBOX and calls gk.resolveEscalation (feedback.js applyItems:312-343 + cli.js apply:74-89). The outbox→inbox bridge is the control-plane SERVER, which exists only in http mode; in file mode nothing moves an emitted outbox event into the inbox, and the shapes even differ (emit builds {type:'decision', payload:{escalationId,decision}} while applyItems consumes top-level {kind:'decision', escalationId, decision}). Consequence: with feedback ENABLED (the recommended config) and no live orchestrator run — or in file mode with no bridging server — resolveEscalation/submitWork return dispatched:true, feedback:'enabled' while the escalation stays OPEN in state.json / the work is never triaged, possibly forever. The result shape gives F8/F10/F12 no field to distinguish 'queued to the control plane, effect pending and conditional on a live run' from the gatekeeper path's synchronous, already-applied mutation — both read dispatched:true. Compounding this, 02-spec.md Open Questions #2 explicitly REQUIRED the tests phase to verify, 'against a live Orky feedback round-trip, that these payload field names survive emit→apply', yet the frozen catalogue (TEST-148..282, TEST-283..290) contains NO live round-trip test: every feedback-path test (TEST-234/237) stubs runCli and asserts only the dispatcher's own dispatched:true, so the primary path's actual effect on state.json was never validated. A human clicking 'resolve' in the eventual F8 UI would be shown success for an action that took no effect.
fix: Either (a) narrow the contract so the feedback path reports a distinct outcome — e.g. dispatched:false with feedback:'enabled' meaning 'submitted to the control plane, will apply when a run processes it', or a dedicated queued/pending-apply signal — so no consumer UI can claim the decision took effect when it was only spooled; or (b) if dispatched:true for a durable emit is intended, document in docs/features/orky-action-dispatch.md that feedback-path dispatch means 'durably QUEUED, applied asynchronously by a running orchestrator, NOT yet reflected in state.json', and add the spec-mandated live emit→apply round-trip test (Open Question #2) so the file-mode / no-live-run no-op is caught rather than assumed. propose CONV: a result flag named for a completed effect (dispatched/applied/resolved) MUST NOT be set true for an action that was only durably QUEUED to an asynchronous transport whose application is deferred and conditional — distinguish 'queued' from 'applied' in the returned shape.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:307-316 (resolveFeatureDir); 03-plan.md TASK-008 ("an existing directory containing (at minimum) a plausible feature marker"); C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/gatekeeper.js:71-80 (loadState) — REQ-005
claim: resolveFeatureDir confirms only statSync(featureDir).isDirectory(); it does NOT check for any feature marker (e.g. state.json), contrary to 03-plan.md TASK-008, which specified it 'confirms it stats as an existing directory containing (at minimum) a plausible feature marker (mirrors validate-root.ts's existence-check style)'. A slug that passes the single-segment shape check and happens to resolve to an existing-but-empty or non-feature directory under <root>/.orky/features/ (a leftover, a shared/template dir, a half-created feature) is accepted as {ok:true} instead of feature-not-found. A subsequent mutating action then runs the CLI against it, and gatekeeper record's loadState (gatekeeper.js:71-80) does NOT throw on a missing state.json — it returns a fabricated default state and saveState then WRITES a brand-new state.json into that directory, materializing a phantom 'feature' plus a recorded human-gate verdict for a feature that never existed; driveStatus against the same dir returns a plausible {next:'await-human'}. REQ-005's acceptance exercises only a truly-nonexistent dir (TEST-232), never the existing-but-empty case, so this gap is untested — a case the spec/tests ignore.
fix: In resolveFeatureDir, additionally require a feature marker (e.g. existsSync(join(featureDir,'state.json')), matching the validate-root.ts '.orky/'-existence style the plan cited) before returning ok; otherwise return feature-not-found. Add a test: a well-formed slug resolving to an existing-but-empty directory under .orky/features/ → feature-not-found with no CLI spawned.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:129-132 (dispose aborts every in-flight AbortController), :320-328 (runWithAbort — the SAME abort wiring is used for mutating record/resolve-escalation/emit CLI calls, not just read-only drive), :150-173/:196-223/:246-257 (mutating queue.run callbacks); src/main/orky/orky-cli-runner.ts:22-39 (execFile passes the signal AND already child.unref()'s); C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/gatekeeper.js:54-57 (writeJson/saveState) and C:/dev/Orky/plugin/feedback/feedback.js:124-131,156-160 (outbox spool) — REQ-010
claim: dispose() SIGTERM-kills in-flight children mid-flight, but the mutating CLIs write NON-ATOMICALLY, so aborting a mutating action mid-write can corrupt the target project's canonical state — and the abort is redundant with the unref() that already prevents the shutdown hang. REQ-010 mandates that an in-flight child be aborted on dispose() (wired via runWithAbort:320-328 -> the AbortController set cleared in dispose:129-132), and this pattern was copied verbatim from src/main/cloud/probe.ts. But probe.ts aborts a READ-ONLY identity query, where a mid-flight kill has zero persistence consequence; F7 applies the same hard-abort to WRITE CLIs. Verified against Orky source: gatekeeper's saveState is `writeJson = fs.mkdirSync(...); fs.writeFileSync(file, JSON.stringify(state))` (gatekeeper.js:54-57) — a non-atomic in-place write of state.json, NOT a temp-then-rename; the feedback file channel likewise spools via a plain `fs.writeFileSync` (feedback.js:124-131 / :156-160). On win32 (the only supported platform) an AbortSignal abort calls child.kill() -> TerminateProcess, which terminates the child immediately at whatever instruction it is executing. If dispose() (wired to owning-window close per plan TASK-010) fires while an in-flight `gatekeeper record`/`resolve-escalation` is inside that writeFileSync, state.json is left truncated/partial — corrupting the ENTIRE feature's pipeline state (all gates, escalations, iterations), the source of truth this whole feature exists to write accurately. Crucially, the abort buys nothing here: orky-cli-runner.ts:38 ALREADY calls child.unref(), so a still-running child never keeps the Electron main process alive nor hangs app.close() (that is REQ-001's stated goal), and the 15s execFile timeout already bounds a runaway child. So for a mutating child, hard-aborting on dispose only ADDS a torn-write corruption risk without a lifecycle benefit — and because the parent exiting does NOT kill an unref()'d child on Windows, simply NOT aborting would let the fast, small write finish cleanly. Low probability (the kill must land inside the sub-millisecond write window of an already-in-flight action at the dispose instant) but high impact (whole-feature state.json corruption in a third-party project). No test exercises abort-during-a-mutating-write (TEST-250 aborts a read-only driveStatus only).
fix: Do not hard-abort in-flight MUTATING children (recordHumanGate/resolveEscalation-fallback/submitWork-emit) on dispose(): they are already unref()'d and timeout-bounded, so let the in-flight write complete (optionally await a short bounded grace in dispose(), or track in-flight mutating promises and await them). Reserve the AbortController hard-kill for the read-only `drive` child, where a mid-flight kill is harmless. Alternatively, gate the abort behind a check that the child has not yet begun its write — but since that instant is unobservable across the process boundary, preferring not-abort for mutating children is the safer default. propose CONV: the abortable/unref()'d execFile pattern (from a read-only probe) MUST NOT be applied unchanged to a subprocess that performs a non-atomic durable write — hard-aborting such a child on dispose/window-close can tear the write; let it finish (it is already unref()'d and timeout-bounded) or make the write atomic before it may be aborted.

location: src/shared/orky-action-result.ts:29-36 (timedOut -> cli-timeout, no data, exitCode null); src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:154-156 (feedback-emit !ok), :169-171 (gatekeeper-fallback !ok), :207-209 (submitWork emit !ok), :253-255 (record !ok) — every one returns dispatched:false on a cli-timeout; C:/dev/Orky/plugin/feedback/feedback.js:72-74 (buildEvent id: rid('EVT')) + :124-131 (unique-per-emit outbox file) — REQ-010, REQ-002, REQ-007, 02-spec.md Open Questions #2
claim: A cli-timeout/abort on a mutating action reports dispatched:false — a DEFINITE non-dispatch — when the true outcome is INDETERMINATE (the write may already have landed), and because the feedback emit is non-idempotent this drives an unsafe duplicate on retry. The result shape has no 'indeterminate/unknown' state: dispatched is a hard boolean and cli-timeout always yields {ok:false, dispatched:false, exitCode:null} (orky-action-result.ts:29-36, threaded through every mutating action's !ok return). But a timeout/abort can fire AFTER the child completed its durable write but before its exit code reached the parent (e.g. dispose-abort landing just after the writeFileSync, or the completed-but-not-yet-collected child). For `feedback emit` this is verifiably non-idempotent: buildEvent assigns a fresh `id: rid('EVT')` per call (feedback.js:72-74) and the file channel spools a new uniquely-named outbox file each emit (:124-131), so a human retrying a submitWork/resolveEscalation after a false-negative timeout produces a SECOND outbox event that a running orchestrator's `feedback apply` tick will process — a duplicate work item / duplicate decision. The dispatcher deliberately (and correctly, given emit's non-idempotency) performs no internal retry, but neither the result contract nor any doc communicates that a cli-timeout on a mutating action is at-most-once/unknown rather than a guaranteed non-dispatch, leaving a downstream UI (F8/F10/F12) or a human free to blindly retry a possibly-applied write. Compounding this, 02-spec.md Open Questions #2 REQUIRED the tests phase to verify emit->apply against a live Orky round-trip; the frozen catalogue contains no such test, so the primary path's actual durability/idempotency was never exercised (every feedback-path test stubs runCli).
fix: Represent an indeterminate outcome distinctly for a cli-timeout/abort on a MUTATING action (resolveEscalation/submitWork/recordHumanGate) — e.g. a dedicated errorKind ('cli-indeterminate' or an `indeterminate:true` flag) or a `dispatched:'unknown'` tri-state — so a consumer/human is warned the effect may or may not have landed and a blind retry is unsafe; keep dispatched:false only for cases proven not to have mutated (rejected pre-CLI). Document in docs/features/orky-action-dispatch.md that the dispatcher intentionally does NOT auto-retry (feedback emit is non-idempotent) and that a timeout on a mutating action is an at-most-once/unknown outcome, and add the spec-mandated live emit->apply round-trip test (Open Question #2). propose CONV: a timeout/abort result for a non-idempotent mutating operation MUST surface the outcome as INDETERMINATE (not a definite non-dispatch) so callers do not blindly retry a possibly-applied write.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:129-132 (dispose — clears controllers but sets no sticky disposed flag), :320-328 (runWithAbort creates a NEW AbortController per call and adds it to the already-cleared set), :150/:196/:246 (the CLI spawn happens INSIDE queue.run, so an action queued behind an in-flight one spawns only after the in-flight one settles) — REQ-001
claim: dispose() is not sticky: it aborts only the AbortControllers that exist at the instant it runs and clears the set, but sets no `disposed` flag, so an action queued behind an in-flight one on the same featureDir will spawn a NEW child AFTER dispose() returned. The mutating actions create their AbortController and spawn the child inside the per-featureDir queue.run callback (runWithAbort). If dispose() runs while action A is in flight and action B is queued behind it on the same normalized featureDir key, dispose aborts A (A resolves as cli-timeout), the queue chain advances, and B's callback then creates a fresh un-aborted controller and spawns a new MUTATING CLI child after the service was disposed — i.e. a durable write to a third-party project can be launched post-dispose / post-window-close. child.unref() means this does not hang app.close(), so REQ-001's 'app close()s without hanging' still holds, but REQ-001's 'after dispose() the service holds zero in-flight children' is only momentarily true and dispose() does not actually prevent subsequent spawns. TEST-250 covers only a single in-flight action, so the queued-behind post-dispose spawn is untested.
fix: Set a `disposed = true` flag in dispose() and short-circuit runWithAbort (and/or the queue.run callbacks) to resolve immediately as aborted/cli-timeout when disposed, so no new child is spawned after dispose(); consider also draining/rejecting queued work on dispose. Add a test: with two same-featureDir mutations in flight, dispose() must ensure the queued-behind action spawns no new child.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-audit.ts:18-30 (OrkyActionAuditRecord shape) and src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:333-347 (recordAudit builds the record); 02-spec.md REQ-013 (enumerated audit fields), lines 55-57 (feedback emit returns `event:<id>`) / 44-45 (gatekeeper record stdout is the recorded gate `{passed, at, ...}`); src/shared/types.ts:423 (OrkyActionResult.feedback)
claim: The audit log — this feature's SOLE compliance/forensics artifact and the stated reason enterprise-arch is a first-class concern (02-spec.md:30-31, 'every dispatch attempt is attributable ... the first place Termhalla mutates a third-party project's state') — records that a mutation was ATTEMPTED and its coarse outcome, but captures NO handle to the durable artifact the mutation actually produced, so an audit line cannot be correlated back to the concrete effect in the target project's control-plane / .orky/ state. (1) Every mutating CLI returns a durable identifier that is thrown away: `feedback emit` returns `{ ok, mode, event:<EVT-id>, sent, spooled }` (02-spec.md:55-57) and `gatekeeper record` returns `{ passed, at, evidence, external }` (02-spec.md:44-45), but recordAudit (orky-action-dispatcher.ts:333-347) copies only `ok/path/dispatched/errorKind/exitCode` from the OrkyActionResult and never reads `result.data`, so the feedback `event` id and the recorded-gate `at` instant are lost. Given a suspected duplicate/lost submission (cf. FINDING-NET-002's non-idempotent-retry concern) an investigator has no way to tie a `dispatched:true, path:'feedback'` audit line to the specific outbox event it emitted. (2) The record also omits the `feedback: 'enabled'|'disabled'` state that OrkyActionResult already carries (src/shared/types.ts:423) — recordAudit never copies `result.feedback` — even though 01-concept.md:39-40 explicitly says 'the caller needs to know WHICH path was actually taken for the audit trail to make sense later'; that state is only weakly re-derivable from `path` and is dropped outright for submitWork's feedback-disabled failure. All of these are redaction-SAFE to capture (an EVT id / an ISO instant / an enum — none is human free-text), so REQ-013's redaction rationale does not justify omitting them. This is NOT a REQ-013 violation (every field REQ-013 literally enumerates IS present) — it is an audit-completeness gap that originates in REQ-013 itself under-specifying forensic correlation for a write-capable surface.
fix: Extend OrkyActionAuditRecord (and recordAudit) to capture a redaction-safe correlation id derived from `result.data` on success — the feedback emit `event` id and/or the gatekeeper recorded-gate `at`/escalation id — plus the `feedback` enabled/disabled state already present on OrkyActionResult, so an audit line can be tied to the exact durable artifact it produced in the target project. Keep free-text bodies redacted (length-only) as today. propose CONV: an audit/attribution record for a durable mutation MUST capture a redaction-safe correlation identifier for the artifact it produced (the transport event id, the recorded timestamp/id), not only the fact and coarse outcome of the attempt, so an audit entry can be reconciled against the mutated system's own state after the fact.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-audit.ts:44-51 (append swallows the write error with console.error only) and src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:348-355 (recordAudit's own defensive try/catch, also console.error only) — REQ-013
claim: An audit-log write failure is swallowed with nothing but a `console.error` at two layers (OrkyActionAuditLog.append:48-50 and recordAudit's guard:351-355), which in a PACKAGED Electron main process has no operator-visible destination — the app ships no structured/persisted logging pipeline (no electron-log, no in-app diagnostic, no counter), so a full disk, a permissions error, or an EMFILE on `orky-actions.jsonl` silently drops audit records with ZERO production visibility. For the app's only write-audit trail on its only third-party-project-mutating surface, silent, unobservable loss of audit records defeats the forensic purpose of keeping the log at all: after an incident an operator cannot even know whether the trail is complete. REQ-013 correctly requires the write to be best-effort (never fail the action) and 'logged' — console.error technically satisfies 'logged' — but best-effort and observable-on-failure are not mutually exclusive; the spec chose the former and left the latter unaddressed. Not a contract violation, but an observability gap on the compliance artifact itself.
fix: Surface an audit-write failure through a durable/observable signal in ADDITION to console.error — e.g. increment a diagnostics counter exposed to the app's status surface, emit a one-time user-visible warning toast on first failure, or route it through a persisted log — while keeping the action itself best-effort (never failing). propose CONV: a best-effort audit/integrity log's write failures MUST be surfaced through an operator-observable signal (a metric/counter, a persisted log, or a one-time notification), never console-only, so silent loss of the audit trail is itself detectable.

location: src/main/orky/orky-cli-locate.ts:15-24 (locateOrkyCli — ORKY_PLUGIN_DIR env var only, no default, no persisted config); src/main/services.ts:53-57 (dispatcher constructed with the default env-bound resolver, no config surface) — REQ-012; 02-spec.md Open questions #1; 03-plan.md Risk note #1
claim: The entire write surface's core external dependency — the on-disk location of Orky's gatekeeper/feedback CLIs — is resolved SOLELY from a process env var `ORKY_PLUGIN_DIR`, with no default and no in-app configuration or diagnostic surface (orky-cli-locate.ts:20-23 returns null when the env var is unset). Operationally this is fragile for a GUI desktop app: an app launched from a Windows shortcut / Start menu inherits the desktop session environment, NOT a shell's, so a user who set `ORKY_PLUGIN_DIR` in their shell profile finds every action fail `orky-cli-not-found` with no in-app way to see or fix the misconfiguration until F13 (Settings) ships a UI. The feature fails HONESTLY and safely (a clear actionable error via describeMissingCli, never a silent no-op or a wrong-machine guess — good), so this is not a defect in delivered behavior, but it does mean the app's first write capability is inoperable out-of-the-box with no self-diagnosis. The config-surface ownership vs F13 is already flagged for the coordinator (02-spec.md Open Q#1, 03-plan.md Risk note #1), but those risk notes do not flow into the findings ledger; filing it here keeps the operability/coordination item tracked for F13.
fix: Confirm the CLI-location config-surface ownership split with F13 (Settings) before F13 is planned so the two don't invent divergent config keys (CONV-012 spirit), and add at least a lightweight in-app diagnostic (surface the resolved-or-unresolved CLI location + the actionable orky-cli-not-found message where a user can see it) so an env-var misconfiguration is self-explaining rather than presenting as a blanket action failure. No change to the single-swappable-resolver design (03-plan.md) is required — only the config/diagnostic surface.

claim: eedback emit internal failures are silently collapsed into the mode:'noop' disabled path. The frozen spec documents that feedback emit always exits 0, with success stdout {ok:true, mode:...} but internal-error stdout {ok:false, mode:'noop', error, note:'emit is non-fatal'} (02-spec.md:54-55), and REQ-011 says feedback emit's mode/ok fields drive REQ-006/REQ-007 and the CLI's own {error} must be surfaced (02-spec.md:293-298). Current mapping ignores the parsed ok:false: every feedback exit 0 becomes {ok:true, data:parsed} in mapCliRunToResult (src/shared/orky-action-result.ts:60-62). The dispatcher then branches only on data.mode: resolveEscalation treats mode:'noop' as feedback disabled and falls back to gatekeeper (src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:157-164), while submitWork reports  eedback-disabled (src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:210-220). Therefore a real feedback CLI internal error such as {ok:false, mode:'noop', error:'disk full'} is returned as a successful gatekeeper fallback if the fallback succeeds, or as  eedback-disabled for submitWork, rather than ok:false/errorKind:'cli-error' carrying the CLI's own error. This violates REQ-002's failure contract (ok:false with actionable error/errorKind for any failure) and REQ-011's explicit mode/ok + {error} handling, and it hides operational feedback failures behind a false disabled/noop diagnosis.
fix: Make the feedback exit-0 mapper inspect the parsed object's ok field before per-action mode handling. If parsed.ok === false, return {ok:false, exitCode:0, errorKind:'cli-error', error:<parsed.error || specific default>} so both resolveEscalation and submitWork surface the feedback CLI failure instead of treating it as disabled. Add dispatcher or mapper tests for emit returning {ok:false, mode:'noop', error:'boom'}: resolveEscalation must not fall back as if feedback were merely disabled, and submitWork must not return  eedback-disabled.
